executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment continues its post-pandemic normalization trajectory, with 74.9 million enrollees reported across 51 states as of February 2026. All major states experienced year-over-year enrollment declines, ranging from North Carolina's flat performance to Michigan's notable 7.6% reduction. Texas, with nearly 4 million enrollees, demonstrated relative stability with a 3.8% decline, outperforming California (-6.8%) and Michigan (-7.6%) but trailing North Carolina and Pennsylvania. National per-enrollee spending reached $2,883 in fiscal year 2024, with significant state variation reflecting demographic and benefit differences. Texas maintained cost efficiency at $3,014 per enrollee, positioning favorably against high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Massachusetts ($4,221), while operating above lower-cost states like Florida ($2,036) and North Carolina ($2,623).

Quality performance reveals concerning disparities, with the national average score of 65.6 masking a substantial performance gap between leading states like Massachusetts (81) and lagging states such as Mississippi (48). Texas scored 62 points, ranking 30th nationally in the "moderate" performance tier, indicating significant opportunity for improvement relative to top-performing states. The state's managed care penetration rate of 93.3% substantially exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for coordinated care delivery that could be leveraged for quality improvements. Recent federal regulatory activity around community engagement requirements, state directed payments, and provider enrollment moratoria signals potential policy shifts that may impact program operations and require strategic planning adjustments across all state programs.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with nearly 4 million enrollees, yet demonstrates a complex performance profile that reflects both operational efficiency and coverage limitations. The state's per-enrollee spending of $3,014 falls modestly above the national average of $2,883 but remains well below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,363) and Ohio ($3,584), while exceeding fellow non-expansion states Florida ($2,036) and North Carolina ($2,623). This spending pattern suggests Texas maintains moderate cost control while potentially limiting benefit generosity. The state's enrollment decline of 3.8% year-over-year aligns closely with the national trend as states complete post-pandemic eligibility redeterminations, though it trails the sharper declines seen in expansion states like Michigan (-7.6%) and Illinois (-5.7%).

Texas's managed care penetration of 93.3% significantly exceeds the national average of 83.2%, indicating strong administrative infrastructure and care coordination capabilities that position the state well for value-based care initiatives. However, the program's quality performance presents a critical challenge, with a score of 62/100 ranking 30th nationally—below the national average of 65.6 and trailing southern peers like North Carolina. This quality gap, combined with Texas's continued resistance to Medicaid expansion, creates a concerning coverage landscape where the state forgoes federal funding while serving a more limited population than comparable large states. Key areas to monitor include upcoming federal regulatory changes around state-directed payments and provider enrollment moratoria, which could impact Texas's managed care operations, as well as potential shifts in the state's expansion stance given the growing fiscal and health outcome pressures.

alerts.title: High Per-Enrollee Spending in PA and MA
alerts.description: Pennsylvania and Massachusetts show per-enrollee spending 51% and 46% above national median respectively, indicating potential cost efficiency issues.

alerts.title: Poor Quality Performance in Southern States
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) have quality scores below 55/100, significantly underperforming the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Low Spending Despite Moderate Quality in FL
alerts.description: Florida shows unusually low per-enrollee spending ($2,036) - 29% below national average - while maintaining moderate quality outcomes.

alerts.title: High Spending with Poor Quality Performance in PA
alerts.description: Pennsylvania combines the highest per-enrollee spending ($4,363) with below-average quality performance, suggesting inefficient resource utilization.

alerts.title: Nationwide Provider Enrollment Moratorium Active
alerts.description: Federal Register shows active temporary moratoriums on provider enrollment across Medicare and Medicaid programs, potentially impacting access to care.

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining 3.8% YoY - potential contract scope reduction
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% year-over-year to 3.997M enrollees, representing a significant membership decline. This is consistent with national trends but creates direct revenue implications for managed care operations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Contract value and scope may be reduced proportionally to enrollment decline, requiring immediate budget and resource planning adjustments. HHSC will need support managing this transition while maintaining service quality.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment decline impact to contract management and initiate scope adjustment discussions with HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score 62/100 ranks #30 nationally - improvement opportunity
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th out of 50 states and falling in the 'moderate' tier. This is significantly below high performers like MA (81) and national leaders.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality improvement represents a major opportunity to strengthen our contract position and demonstrate value. Poor quality scores risk regulatory scrutiny and contract penalties.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement initiatives and benchmark against top 5 performing states' best practices

executiveInsights.title: Nationwide provider enrollment moratorium announced - operational impact
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced nationwide temporary moratoria on provider enrollment across Medicare, Medicaid, and CHIP programs. Multiple Federal Register notices indicate significant regulatory enforcement activity.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Provider network adequacy and new provider onboarding will be constrained, potentially impacting service delivery and contract performance metrics. HHSC needs immediate guidance on compliance.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag provider enrollment moratorium to HHSC leadership and assess network adequacy contingency plans

executiveInsights.title: Texas $3,014 per-enrollee cost 4.5% above national average of $2,883
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $3,014 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,883, indicating 4.5% higher costs. Total Texas expenditures reach $12.048B annually.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs suggest potential efficiency opportunities or reflect higher acuity populations. This cost differential impacts budget planning and positions us to demonstrate cost management value.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review cost drivers analysis and benchmark Texas efficiency metrics against lower-cost states like FL and NC

executiveInsights.title: Texas 93.3% managed care penetration exceeds national 83.2% average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3.86M MC enrollees, significantly higher than the national average of 83.2%. This represents strong managed care market maturity.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High managed care penetration indicates sophisticated infrastructure and experience that positions Texas as a potential model for other states. This strengthens our competitive position for similar contracts.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Document Texas managed care best practices for replication in other state opportunities

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.8% YoY, risking reduced contract values and operational scope.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost-efficient service delivery models to maintain margins despite lower enrollment.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality performance gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in performance-based contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement initiative to move Texas into top-tier performance.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care dominance
riskOpportunity.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration, well above national average, indicating strong digitization opportunities.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Leverage high MC penetration to propose advanced analytics and automation solutions.

riskOpportunity.label: Federal regulatory changes
riskOpportunity.description: Multiple pending CMS regulations on managed care payments and provider enrollment create compliance risks.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish regulatory compliance consulting practice to help Texas navigate federal changes.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG work plan items on Medicaid create audit and compliance risk exposure.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement proactive compliance monitoring and audit preparation protocols.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC procurement pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Texas HHSC maintains active procurement opportunities portal indicating potential contract expansions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated business development team to monitor and respond to HHSC opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas spends $3,014 per enrollee versus national average of $2,883, indicating potential optimization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose cost optimization initiatives targeting the $131 per-enrollee gap versus national average.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: Federal temporary moratorium on provider enrollment could disrupt network adequacy and operations.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop contingency plans for provider network management during enrollment restrictions.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 3,997,752 Medicaid enrollees as of February 2026.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $3,014 per Medicaid enrollee in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks 30th nationally with a moderate-tier quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement opportunities tracked across HHSC and ESBD portals.

pulseMetrics.tooltip: Nine federal regulatory items affecting Medicaid operations are currently active.
